rows.title: CDMG
rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[l_quotient_vs_ordered_pair_typing_inconsistent] The set $L$ of bidirected edges may be encoded either as a subset of the quotient $V \times V / {\sim}$ (with $(v_1, v_2) \sim (v_2, v_1)$, i.e., as unordered pairs) or as a subset of $V \times V$ subject to the symmetry constraint $(v_1, v_2) \in L \iff (v_2, v_1) \in L$. Both encodings are admissible; in the ordered-pair encoding, membership of $(v_1, v_2)$ and $(v_2, v_1)$ must always coincide. The irreflexivity condition $v_1 \neq v_2$ applies under either encoding.

[edge_set_disjointness_under_specified] The qualifier "disjoint" applied to the edge sets $E$ and $L$ is to be read as trivial type-level disjointness — directed edges and bidirected edges are objects of distinct types — and imposes no graph-theoretic mutual-exclusion constraint between them. In particular, for $v_1, v_2 \in V$, it is permitted to have $(v_1, v_2) \in E$ and $\{v_1, v_2\} \in L$ simultaneously; if a formalization encodes both $E$ and $L$ as subsets of ordered pairs over $V$, the same ordered pair $(v, w)$ may belong to both $E$ and $L$.

[manual_1] The vertex sets $J$ and $V$ are both finite.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: bidirected_edges_quotient_vs_symmetry_redundancy
explanation: The bidirected edge set is declared as `L \ins V \times V/((v_1,v_2) \sim (v_2,v_1))`, i.e. a subset of the quotient of `V × V` by the swap relation. Elements of this quotient are unordered pairs (equivalence classes). But the accompanying condition is written as

    "$(v_1,v_2) \in L \, \implies\, v_1\neq v_2 \land (v_2,v_1) \in L$"

which treats elements of `L` as if they were ordered pairs.

If `L` really is a subset of the quotient, then `(v_1, v_2)` and `(v_2, v_1)` denote the *same* equivalence class, so "$(v_2, v_1) \in L$" follows automatically from "$(v_1, v_2) \in L$" — that half of the conjunction is vacuous. Only the `v_1 ≠ v_2` clause (no self-loops in `L`) carries content.

Alternatively, if the author actually intends `L \subseteq V \times V` (no quotient) with the implication forcing symmetry, then the quotient notation is misleading — it suggests a different ambient set.

This ambiguity matters for any encoding: should `L` be a `Sym2 V`, a `Finset (V × V)` with a symmetry axiom, or an irreflexive symmetric relation? The LN simultaneously suggests two of these.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: directed_self_loops_allowed_but_bidirected_self_loops_forbidden
explanation: The constraint `v_1 \neq v_2` is imposed on `L` (no bidirected self-loops), but `E` has no analogous constraint. The literal definition `E \ins (J \cup V) \times V` permits a pair `(v, v)` with `v \in V`, since then `v \in J \cup V` and `v \in V`. So a directed self-loop `v \to v` on an output node is admissible, while a bidirected self-loop `v \leftrightarrow v` is explicitly forbidden.

This asymmetry between E and L may be intentional (e.g. directed self-loops sometimes encode feedback) but the LN doesn't justify or even acknowledge it. A reader scanning the definition could easily assume self-loops are uniformly disallowed. Downstream notions like acyclicity (def_3_6) will need to take a stance on whether `v \to v` counts as a length-1 cycle.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: empty_cdmg_admitted_by_active_definition
explanation: The active text imposes no non-emptiness condition on `J \cup V`. Hence the literal definition admits the fully empty CDMG with `J = V = E = L = \emptyset`. This is a classic degenerate corner case: any downstream definition that implicitly assumes a non-empty vertex set (walks of positive length, "the source of a directed edge", topological orders on non-empty supports, etc.) needs to either gracefully handle, or explicitly exclude, the empty graph. Worth flagging because future claims/proofs that quantify "for every CDMG …" will quietly include this trivial case.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: CDMGNotation
rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[sus_omits_tail_tail_despite_star_eq_head_or_tail] CDMGs do not admit a tail-tail edge type; consequently the placeholder rule "the star stands for arrowhead or tail" yields only the three enumerated configurations in item 7 (the tail-tail case "$v_1 \tut v_2$" is excluded by definition, not omitted by oversight). Any case analysis on "$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$" may assume exhaustiveness over exactly the three listed alternatives $\{\tuh, \hut, \huh\}$.

[huh_visual_symmetry_vs_ordered_pair_in_L] The relation $L$ is required to be symmetric: for all $v_1, v_2$, $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ if and only if $(v_2, v_1) \in L$. Consequently, $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \iff v_2 \huh v_1 \in G$, and the symmetry of the $\huh$-disjunct propagates to items 5–7 accordingly.

wording_check.output:
I've reviewed the tex block carefully and cross-checked the CDMG definition (def_3_1) and the usage of `\sus` in def_3_3 (as "adjacent").

```
SUBTLETY:
id: star_placeholder_omits_tail_tail_in_sus
explanation: The trailing sentence says "The star stands for a placeholder to mean: ``arrowhead or tail''.''
Read literally, this is a meta-rule: any star in $\suh$, $\hus$, $\sus$ can be expanded to either "arrowhead" (i.e.\ head) or "tail". Mechanically applying that rule to $\sus$ gives four combinations for (left, right):

  (tail, tail) — $\tut$
  (tail, head) — $\tuh$
  (head, tail) — $\hut$
  (head, head) — $\huh$

But the actual enumeration in the definition lists only three of these:

  ``$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$''

The fourth case, $\tut$ (tail--tail), is silently omitted. The omission is consistent with the CDMG edge inventory of def_3_1 — directed edges in $E$ have one tail and one head, bidirected edges in $L$ have two heads, and no edge type has two tails — so $\tut$ is vacuous as an edge of $G$ regardless. So the enumeration is semantically correct *for CDMG*.

However, the literal text creates a mild internal inconsistency: the meta-description ("star = arrowhead or tail") predicts 4 cases for $\sus$, while the enumeration gives 3. A reader (or a formalizer mechanically unfolding the placeholder) would expect either (a) the meta-rule to qualify itself with "...such that the resulting edge type exists in the graph", or (b) an explicit tail--tail clause that resolves to $\bot$.

A concrete corner case: a Lean encoding that defines $\mathrm{star}$ as an inductive with two constructors {arrowhead, tail} and then defines $\sus$ by pattern-matching all $2\times 2 = 4$ combinations would faithfully follow the meta-rule but would need a fourth (vacuous) case the LN doesn't spell out. Conversely, an encoding that hard-codes only the three listed cases is faithful to the enumeration but not to the meta-description.

Downstream, def_3_3 uses $\sus$ to define adjacency. The 3-case enumeration matches the intended "any edge between $v_1$ and $v_2$" meaning, so the substantive content is fine; the subtlety is purely in the wording of the placeholder explanation versus the explicit list.
END_SUBTLETY
```

rows.title: EdgeRelations
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [inconsistent_writing_order_enumeration_into_vs_out_of] Throughout the enumeration above, the writings $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ denote the same edge (and the bidirected writings $v_1 \huh v_2$ and $v_2 \huh v_1$ likewise coincide). Membership of an edge in the categories "into $v$" or "out of $v$" depends only on the underlying element of $E$, not on which of these textual writings is used; item 3's explicit listing of both writings and item 2's single-writing enumeration are therefore equivalent under this identification.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: out_of_v2_not_literally_defined
explanation: Item 2 separately defines "into $v_1$" and "into $v_2$" using a fixed edge ordering ($v_1 \star v_2$) and gives two distinct cases. Item 3, by contrast, only defines "out of $v_1$" and absorbs the two orderings of writing the edge into a single case: "$v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_2 \hut v_1$". The literal text never defines "out of $v_2$" at all. By analogy a reader would expect "out of $v_2$" to be: edges of the form $v_1 \hut v_2$ or $v_2 \tuh v_1$ — but that has to be supplied by the reader; it is not in the tex. A pedantic formalizer will encode "out of $v_1$" exactly as written and find that downstream claims that range over arbitrary vertices ("for every $v \in V$, the edges out of $v$ …") strictly speaking only cover the case where $v$ has been bound to the first label of the edge.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: bidirected_huh_into_both_out_of_neither
explanation: Under the literal reading of item 2, the bidirected edge $v_1 \huh v_2$ is "into $v_1$" AND "into $v_2$". Under item 3, no $\huh$-edge ever counts as "out of $v_1$" — the enumerated forms are only $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$. So a $\huh$-edge is "into both endpoints" but "out of neither". This is consistent with CDMG semantics (a bidirected edge denotes latent confounding, with no directed influence either way), but it has a counter-intuitive consequence: "into" and "out of" are NOT complementary classifications of incident edges. A reader or formalizer who treats "out of $v$" as shorthand for "incident at $v$ but not into $v$" will get the wrong answer for $\huh$-edges — they are "into" and "not out of". This matters for any downstream notion built on "out of" (out-degree, children, directed-out-neighbourhood), where bidirected edges must contribute zero, not "the remainder after removing in-edges". Corner case: if a vertex $v$ has only $\huh$-edges incident to it, then $v$ has no out-edges at all, despite having non-zero adjacency.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: CDMGRestrictions
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [implicit_universal_quantifier_in_hus_clause] The statements "$j \hus v \notin G$" and "edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed" carry implicit universal quantification over $v$. Precisely: for every $j \in J$ and every node $v$ in the graph, $j \hus v \notin G$; and for every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, an edge $j \tuh v$ is permitted in $G$ (its presence is not asserted, only that no restriction forbids it).

wording_check.output:
I now have full context. Let me audit each statement of the remark carefully against the CDMG definition and notation.

**Statement 1**: "$j \hus v \notin G$" — given $\hus = \hut$ or $\huh$. For $j \hut v$: needs $(v,j) \in E \ins (J\cup V) \times V$, forcing $j \in V$, contradicts $j \in J$. For $j \huh v$: needs $(j,v) \in L \ins V \times V$, again forces $j \in V$. ✓

**Statement 2**: "$j \tuh v$ are allowed" with $v \in V$ — directly matches $E$'s codomain constraint. ✓

**Statement 3**: "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent" — follows from edge-type constraints (no $j_1 \tuh j_2$ since target must be in $V$; no $j_1 \huh j_2$ since both ends must be in $V$). ✓

Corner cases ($J = \emptyset$, $V = \emptyset$, singleton $J$) all check out vacuously.

The variable $v$ in statement 1 is implicitly universally quantified — standard math-text convention (and the not-cdmg bullets confirm $v$ defaults to ranging over $G = J \cup V$). The mild inconsistency where statement 2 re-binds $v$ to $V$ explicitly does not create a substantive ambiguity since both readings yield equivalent truth values (the J-J cases are excluded by the edge-set definitions regardless). The "Furthermore" introduces a consequence, not new content — but the remark itself is a derived characterization, so that's expected.

NO_SUBTLETIES

rows.title: Walks
rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Walks]\label{def:walks}
     Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $v,w \in G$.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item A \emph{walk} from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a finite alternating sequence of adjacent nodes and edges 
      \[v=v_0, a_0,  v_1, \dots v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n=w\] 
%      \[v=v_0 \sus v_1 \sus  \cdots \sus v_{n-1} \sus v_n=w\]
        in $G$ for some $n \ge 0$, i.e.\ such that for every $k=0,\dots,n-1$ 
        we have that $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \cup L$ or $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$, and with end nodes $v_0=v$ and $v_n=w$.
        An example walk from $v_0$ to $v_3$ could look like:
        \[v_0 \tuh v_1 \hut  v_2 \huh v_3, \qquad\text{ with } \qquad  v_0 \tuh v_1, v_2 \tuh v_1 \in E,\,v_2 \huh v_3 \in L. \]
        The same node may appear multiple times in a walk.
        Also the \emph{trivial walk} consisting of a single node $v_0 \in G$ is allowed (if $v=w$).
        The walk is called \emph{into $v_0$} if $a_0 = v_0 \hus v_1$, and \emph{out of $v_0$} if $a_0 = v_0 \tuh v_1$.
        Similarly, it is called \emph{into $v_n$} if $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \suh v_n$ and \emph{out of $v_n$} if $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \hut v_n$.
    \item A \emph{directed walk} from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form:
        \[v=v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh  \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\]
        for some $n \ge 0$,
        where all arrowheads point in the direction of $w$ and there are no arrowheads pointing back.
   % \item[] Directed walks exclude the trivial walk per definition.
    \item A \emph{bidirected walk} from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form:
        \[v=v_0 \huh v_1 \huh  \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh v_n=w,\]
        for some $n \ge 0$, where all edges are bidirected.
    \item A \emph{collider walk} from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form:
        \[v=v_0 \suh v_1 \huh  \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus v_n=w,\]
        for some $n \ge 0$, where all nodes in between $v$ and $w$ have two arrowheads pointing towards them (a.k.a.\ collider).
        Note that for $n=1$ this reads: $v\sus w \in G$.
    \item A walk is called \emph{path} if no node occurs more than once.
    \item A \emph{bifurcation} between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ is a walk of the form:
        \[v=v_0 \hut v_1 \hut  \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \hus v_k  \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\]
   %             \[v=v_0 \hut v_1 \hut  \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \huh v_k  \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\]
        such that $v \ne w$, the walk contains both endnodes exactly once, 
	the subwalk $v_0 \hut \cdots v_{k-1}$ is a directed walk from $v_{k-1}$ to $v_0$,
	and the subwalk $v_k \tuh \cdots v_n$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_n$.
        %every node has at most one arrowhead pointing towards it, and 
        %both endnodes have exactly one arrowhead pointing towards them. 
        If the edge $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$ is directed ($v_{k-1} \hut v_k$) then we say that the bifurcation has \emph{source} $v_{k}$.
  \end{enumerate}
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}
rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[bifurcation_right_chain_trivial_is_just_directed_walk] As an additional constraint on the bifurcation form, both endnodes $v_0$ and $v_n$ must have exactly one arrowhead pointing towards them. In particular, this excludes the degenerate case $k = n$ with directed hinge $v_{n-1} \hut v_n$, in which the walk reduces to a directed walk $v_0 \leftarrow v_1 \leftarrow \cdots \leftarrow v_n$ and $v_n$ has no arrowhead pointing towards it.

[collider_walk_n1_form_contradicts_inline_note] For $n=1$, a collider walk from $v$ to $w$ requires a bidirected edge $v \huh w \in E$ (arrowheads at both endpoints), as dictated by the form $v_0 \suh v_1$ combined with $v_{n-1} \hus v_n$ collapsing onto the single edge. The inline note "$v \sus w \in G$" is to be read in this stricter sense and does *not* admit purely directed edges $v \tuh w$ or $v \hut w$ as collider walks of length 1.

wording_check.output:
Looking at the LN text for def_3_4 carefully, I'll examine each item for subtleties.

The walk types admit `n ≥ 0` (so trivial walks count). "Into/out of" is defined symbolically using $\hus$, $\tuh$, etc. The collider walk has an explicit $n=1$ clarification. The bifurcation pattern allows wide ranges of $k$ and $n$.

Let me identify several genuine issues:

```
SUBTLETY:
id: collider_walk_n1_literal_pattern_vs_note
explanation: The collider-walk pattern
  "v=v_0 \suh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus v_n=w"
for n=1 literally has v_{n-1}=v_0 and v_n=v_1, so the single edge between v_0 and v_1 must satisfy *both* the "first-slot" constraint $v_0 \suh v_1$ (any-tail at v_0, arrowhead at v_1) and the "last-slot" constraint $v_{n-1} \hus v_n = v_0 \hus v_1$ (arrowhead at v_0, any at v_1). Combined, this forces the lone edge to be bidirected ($v_0 \huh v_1$).
However, the LN explicitly states: "Note that for $n=1$ this reads: $v\sus w \in G$" — i.e., any edge between v and w qualifies.
So the symbolic pattern, read literally, is strictly more restrictive than the author's stated meaning. The author is silently relying on a "the $\suh$/$\hus$ marks only constrain the side facing interior colliders" reading, which is also consistent with the verbal description "all nodes in between $v$ and $w$ have two arrowheads pointing towards them" (for n=1 there are no in-between nodes, so no constraint). A naive formalizer who follows the literal pattern would reject e.g. $v_0 \tuh v_1$ as a length-1 collider walk; the author's note says it should be accepted. Downstream, this matters wherever "collider walk of length 1 from v to w" is used as a synonym for "v and w are adjacent in any edge type".
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: bifurcation_admits_n1_k1_single_backward_or_bidirected_edge
explanation: The bifurcation pattern
  "v=v_0 \hut v_1 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \hus v_k \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w"
imposes only $1 \le k \le n$ (so that $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ exist), $v \ne w$, "the walk contains both endnodes exactly once", and that the two subwalks are directed walks. The directed-walk subclass admits $n \ge 0$, so the *trivial* subwalk consisting of a single node *is* a directed walk.

Consequence: for n=1, k=1, the left subwalk is just the node $v_0$ (trivial directed walk from $v_0$ to $v_0$), the right subwalk is just $v_1$ (trivial directed walk from $v_1$ to $v_1$), and the entire bifurcation collapses to the single edge $v_0 \hus v_1$. The $\hus$ admits:
  - $v_0 \hut v_1$, i.e. the directed edge $w \to v$ ("backward" relative to the bifurcation's stated direction "between $v$ and $w$");
  - $v_0 \huh v_1$, i.e. a single bidirected edge.

Neither of these visually "bifurcates" — there is no node from which two distinct directed walks emanate. A single arrow $w \to v$ is literally called a "bifurcation between $v$ and $w$" by the definition, which seems to contradict the intuitive meaning of the word. The same degeneracy occurs more generally whenever $k=1$ alone or $k=n$ alone: one of the two arms is empty, so the structure is just a directed walk plus a backward/bidirected edge at one end, not a true Y-shape.

Downstream claims that rely on "a bifurcation has two distinct directed walks emanating from a common node" would fail for these degenerate instances unless the definition is tightened (e.g. requiring $1 < k < n$, or equivalently that each subwalk be non-trivial).
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: bifurcation_source_can_be_endnode
explanation: The final sentence says: "If the edge $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$ is directed ($v_{k-1} \hut v_k$) then we say that the bifurcation has source $v_k$."
The "source" is intuitively the apex of the Y-shape — a node strictly between the two endpoints with outgoing arrows toward both. But the literal definition assigns "source $= v_k$" whenever the middle edge is directed, regardless of whether $v_k$ is an internal node or an endpoint.

Concrete corner case: when $k=n$, $v_k = v_n = w$ — so the source of the bifurcation is the endnode $w$ itself. For example, $n=2, k=2$ gives the walk $v_0 \hut v_1 \hut v_2$ (i.e. $v_2 \to v_1 \to v_0$), and the LN calls this a "bifurcation between $v_0$ and $v_2$ with source $v_2$" — but this is literally just a directed walk from $v_2$ to $v_0$ with no branching. The endpoint $w=v_2$ is declared the "source" even though it has only one outgoing arrow (toward $v_1$), not two.

Similarly, when $n=k=1$ with a directed middle edge $v_0 \hut v_1$ (i.e. $w \to v$), the source is again the endnode $w$.

This makes "source of a bifurcation" potentially equal to one of the named endnodes $v$ or $w$, which is surprising terminology and could cause confusion in downstream proofs that treat the source as a distinguished interior node.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: into_out_of_undefined_for_trivial_walk
explanation: The "into/out of" classifications are defined via $a_0$ and $a_{n-1}$:
  - "into $v_0$" iff $a_0 = v_0 \hus v_1$
  - "out of $v_0$" iff $a_0 = v_0 \tuh v_1$
  - "into $v_n$" iff $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \suh v_n$
  - "out of $v_n$" iff $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \hut v_n$

For the trivial walk ($n=0$, single node $v_0=v=w$), neither $a_0$ nor $a_{n-1}$ exists, so the walk is *neither* into nor out of its (single) endpoint, on either side. The LN does not explicitly address this; it's vacuous by the symbolic definitions.

This is fine in isolation, but downstream lemmas that treat "into/out of" as a dichotomy (e.g. claiming "every walk from $v$ to $w$ is either into $v_0$ or out of $v_0$") would break for the trivial walk. A formalizer should be aware that these four predicates can all be simultaneously false on a walk.

Also worth noting: for bidirected first edges ($a_0 = v_0 \huh v_1$), the walk is "into $v_0$" (because $\hus$ matches $\huh$) but NOT "out of $v_0$" (because $\tuh$ requires a tail at $v_0$). So "into" and "out of" are mutually exclusive but not exhaustive even for non-trivial walks — bidirected ends are "into" only.
END_SUBTLETY
```

rows.title: FamilyRelationships
rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Family relationships]
     Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $v,w \in V$ and $A \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes.
     We then define:
     \begin{enumerate}
         \item The set of \emph{parents} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, w \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
         \item[] The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).\]
         %\item[] \PF{maybe better for sets (???) :The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \lp \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v)\rp \sm A.\]
         %    but then  if $v \tuh v \in E$ then $v \notin \Pa^G(\lC v\rC ) \neq \Pa^G(v) \ni v$ ... }
         \item The set of \emph{children} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
         \item[] The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
         \item The set of \emph{siblings} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Sib^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \huh w \in G \rC.\]
         %\item[] The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
         \item The set of \emph{ancestors} of $v$ in $G$: 
             \[\Anc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } w \tuh \cdots \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The set of \emph{ancestors} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$.
         \item The set of \emph{descendants} of $v$ in $G$: 
             \[\Desc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } v \tuh \cdots \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v \in \Desc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The set of \emph{descendants} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$.
         \item The set of \emph{non-descendants} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).\]
         \item The \emph{strongly connected component} of $v$ in $G$:
             \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).\]
             Note: $v \in \Sc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The (union of) \emph{strongly connected components} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$.
         \item The \emph{district} of $v$ in $G$:

             \[\Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v).\]

rows.title: Acyclicity
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [nontrivial_directed_walk_not_defined_in_block] A non-trivial directed walk is a directed walk $v_0 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n$ with $n \ge 1$ (i.e., one that traverses at least one edge); the trivial walk consisting of a single node $v_0$ is excluded. In particular, a self-loop $v \tuh v$ constitutes a non-trivial directed walk from $v$ to itself, so an acyclic CDMG contains no self-loops on any $v \in V$.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: non_trivial_walk_undefined_admits_self_loop_and_2cycle_ambiguity
explanation: The block uses "non-trivial directed walk from $v$ to itself" without defining "non-trivial" anywhere in this definition. Two plausible readings:

  (a) **Length ≥ 1**: any closed walk other than the zero-length walk at $v$. Under this reading, a self-loop $v \to v$ is itself a non-trivial walk from $v$ to $v$, so acyclicity forbids self-loops, and a 2-cycle $v \to w \to v$ is also forbidden.

  (b) **Length ≥ 2** (or "passes through a vertex other than $v$"): self-loops would not count as non-trivial, so a CDMG consisting of just $v \to v$ would still be "acyclic" under this reading. Some authors use "non-trivial" this way when they want to single out "real" cycles.

  The standard graph-theoretic intent is almost certainly (a), but the LN never says so. This becomes a real corner-case question whenever the rest of the LN reasons about acyclic CDMGs: for instance, any later argument that invokes a topological ordering of $V$ implicitly needs self-loops excluded, i.e. reading (a). Conversely, any later claim that an acyclic CDMG may have $v \to v$ as a benign self-edge would force reading (b). Without an explicit anchor, downstream proofs can silently rely on whichever convention the formalizer chose.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: node_quantifier_scope_v_in_G_unclear_J_versus_V
explanation: The quantifier reads "for any node $v \in G$", but $G = (J, V, E, L)$ is a 4-tuple, not a set of nodes. The literal text leaves it unclear whether $v$ ranges over

  (i) just $V$, or
  (ii) $V \cup J$ (the full node set, assuming $J$ also consists of nodes).

  In a CDMG, $J$ typically plays a distinguished role (e.g., context/parameter/index nodes that do not receive directed edges, or that are treated structurally differently from $V$). If acyclicity is meant to be a condition only on the endogenous part $V$, the definition should say "$v \in V$"; if it's meant to apply to all nodes regardless of role, it should say "$v \in V \cup J$" (and even then $v \in G$ is sloppy).

  Corner case: suppose a node $j \in J$ has only outgoing edges (by construction of CDMG). Then no walk from $j$ to $j$ exists anyway, so under reading (ii) the condition is trivially satisfied at $j$ — no practical difference. But if the CDMG model permits some incoming structure at $J$, the two readings genuinely diverge: a $j$-to-$j$ walk could exist and would only be forbidden under reading (ii). A formalizer must pick one; the LN does not say which.

wording_check.output:

id: equivalent_indexing_assumes_finite_node_set
explanation: The LN gives a primary definition — "a total order $<$ of $J \cup V$ such that for all $v,w \in G$: $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$" — and then claims "Equivalently, it can be described as an indexing of the nodes $J \cup V = \{v_1,\dots,v_K\}$ where parents always precede their children." The two formulations are equivalent only when $J \cup V$ is finite (so that the strict total order has order type a finite ordinal and can be written as $v_1 < v_2 < \dots < v_K$). For an infinite node set, the primary definition is still meaningful (any total order on an infinite set is fine), but the "indexing" reformulation either fails entirely (uncountable case) or requires further conditions (the order type must be $\omega$ for a countable set). The definition does not state a finiteness hypothesis on $J \cup V$ — if CDMGs are taken to be finite implicitly (likely the intended convention), the equivalence is fine; if not, the "Equivalently" clause is genuinely stronger than the primary definition. A downstream argument that picks an index `i` and inducts on the index relies on the indexing formulation and would silently inherit a finiteness assumption.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: quantifier_domain_v_w_in_G_is_tuple_not_set
explanation: The implication is stated "for all $v,w \in G$", but $G = (J,V,E,L)$ is literally a 4-tuple, not a set, so "$v \in G$" does not type-check on the nose. The clearly intended domain is $J \cup V$ (matching "a total order $<$ of $J \cup V$" in the previous clause, and matching what makes "$v < w$" defined). This is conventional graph-theoretic shorthand and unambiguous in intent, but a formalization must commit to the domain explicitly — and in particular must ensure that whenever $v \in \Pa^G(w)$, $v$ is in fact in $J \cup V$ so that the comparison $v < w$ is defined. (For a CDMG this should follow from the definition of $\Pa^G$, but it is a side-condition the LN never spells out.)
END_SUBTLETY

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: bidirected_edge_removal_assumes_symmetric_representation
explanation: The bidirected-edge removal set is written as
\[
L_{\doit(W)} := L \sm \lC v \huh w \,|\, v \in G, w \in W \rC,
\]
which literally picks out bidirected edges whose *second* endpoint is in $W$. The notation $\huh$ (head-to-head) connotes symmetry, but the LN never says whether $L$ is stored as an unordered set of pairs (in which case $v \huh w$ and $w \huh v$ denote the same element and the removal is symmetric) or as an ordered/directed-representation set (in which case the formula only matches edges where the in-$W$ endpoint sits in the second slot).

Corner case: suppose $L$ contains the ordered tuple $(w, v)$ where $w \in W$ and $v \notin W$. With ordered-pair semantics, the removal pattern requires the second component to be in $W$, so $(w,v)$ would *not* be matched and the bidirected edge incident to $w$ would survive the intervention. With unordered/symmetric semantics, it is matched. The literal text leaves this open.

This matters because the natural-language gloss "remove all edges into nodes from $W$" suggests the author intends "remove every bidirected edge incident to $W$", which is the symmetric reading — but the formula does not symmetrize, so any downstream claim of the form "the intervened CDMG has no bidirected edge with an endpoint in $W$" silently relies on the symmetry of $L$.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: intervention_on_already_input_nodes_admits_nontrivial_edge_changes
explanation: The hypothesis is $W \ins J \cup V$, so $W$ is allowed to contain nodes that are already in $J$ (i.e.\ already input nodes). For the node sets this is harmless and idempotent: $J_{\doit(W)} = J \cup W$, $V_{\doit(W)} = V \sm W$ both leave $W \cap J$ untouched. But the edge removal sets do *not* see "already in $J$" as a special case — they still strip every $v \tuh w$ and $v \huh w$ with $w \in W \cap J$.

Corner case: take $W = \{j\}$ with $j \in J$. Conceptually one would expect "intervening on a node that is already exogenous" to be a no-op. Structurally it is a no-op for $(J, V)$, but if the underlying CDMG admits any bidirected edge $v \huh j$ with $v \neq j$ (a confounding link incident to an input), the literal definition deletes it. The natural-language summary "we turn all nodes from $W$ into input nodes and remove all edges into nodes from $W$" elides this case — it reads as describing a conversion that does not apply to nodes already in $J$, but the formal removal still fires on them.

If a downstream lemma asserts something like "intervening twice on the same set is the same as intervening once" or "intervening on a subset of $J$ leaves $G$ unchanged", it would have to be reconciled with this asymmetry between the node-set rule (idempotent on $J$) and the edge-set rule (not idempotent in the presence of bidirected edges touching $J$).
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: v_in_G_is_informal_notation
explanation: Both removal sets use the quantifier "$v \in G$", e.g.\ $\lC v \tuh w \,|\, v \in G, w \in W \rC$. Strictly, $G = (J, V, E, L)$ is a 4-tuple, not a set, so "$v \in G$" has no formal meaning. The intended reading is presumably $v \in J \cup V$ (any node of $G$), but the literal text does not say so. This matters mildly because it determines whether $v = w$ is allowed (self-loops $w \tuh w$, $w \huh w$ with $w \in W$): under the charitable reading $v \in J \cup V$, self-loops are included in the removal set; under a stricter reading they might not be. The LN does not pin this down.
END_SUBTLETY

$= v_n = w$.

Concrete corner case: in an acyclic CDMG with a single directed edge
$w \to v$, def 3.4 admits this as a bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ with
source $w$ (take $n = 1$, $k = 1$: left arm trivial $v_0 = v$, right arm
trivial $v_1 = w$, middle edge $v_0 \hut v_1$, source $v_1 = w$). The RHS of
this proposition, with $c = w$, requires $w \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}$
— i.e., $w$ is a strict ancestor of itself in $G_{\doit(v)}$ — which is
impossible in an acyclic graph. So LHS = TRUE, RHS = FALSE.

The discrepancy is even sharper when $w \in J$: by claim 3.1, $J$-vertices
have no incoming edges, so $\Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) = \{w\}$ unconditionally,
making $\sm \{w\} = \emptyset$ — yet any $w \in J$ with $w \to v$ trivially
yields a "bifurcation with source $w$" under def 3.4.

The accompanying proof papers over this: "A bifurcation between $v$ and $w$
with source $c$ is a walk in $G$ of the form $v \hut \cdots \hut c \tuh \cdots \tuh w$"
tacitly reads "$\cdots$" as non-empty on both sides — but def 3.4 does not
impose that.

There is also a related asymmetry worth flagging: def 3.4 fixes $v_0 = v$ and
$v_n = w$, so source $= v_k$ with $k \ge 1$ excludes $c = v$ but allows
$c = w$ (when $k = n$); yet this proposition's RHS is fully symmetric in $v$
and $w$. So the RHS is strictly stronger than def 3.4 on the $w$-end and
exactly aligns on the $v$-end.

Resolution requires picking one convention: either def 3.4 implicitly demands
$1 \le k \le n-1$ (strictly internal source), in which case this proposition
is correct but def 3.4 needs the constraint made explicit; or source can
coincide with $w$, in which case this proposition's RHS needs an extra
disjunct (e.g. allow $c = w$ when $w \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\}$).
Downstream uses that conclude "no bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ with
source $c$" from the RHS being false will, under the permissive reading,
silently miss the $w \to v$ edge case.
END_SUBTLETY
```

rows.title: NodeSplittingOn
rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Node-splitting on CDMGs]
  \label{def:G_node-splitting}
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W \ins V$ a subset of the output nodes.
    The \emph{node-split graph} w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CDMG: 
    \[ G_{\spl(W)} :=\lp J_{\spl(W)}, V_{\spl(W)}, E_{\spl(W)},L_{\spl(W)} \rp,\]
    %\[ G_{\spl(W)} := G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    %\[G((V\sm W) \dcup W^o|\doit(W^i \dcup J)):=G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    constructed as follows.
    We first make two disjont copies of the nodes in $W$:
    \[ W^0:=\lC w^0\st w \in W \rC, \qquad W^1:=\lC w^1 \st w \in W \rC.  \]
    Note that we consider $w^0 \neq w^1$ for $w \in W$.
    Additionally (for convenience), for $v \in J \cup V \sm W$ we put:
  \[ v^0:=v^1:=v.  \]
  We then define:
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
      \item $J_{\spl(W)} := J$,
      \item $V_{\spl(W)} := (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1$, %which could be identified with $V$ again if we want to make the identification $W=W^0$.
      \item $E_{\spl(W)} := \lC v^1_1 \tuh v_2^0 \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \cup \lC w^0 \tuh w^1 \st w \in W \rC$,
      \item $L_{\spl(W)} :=\lC v_1^0 \huh v_2^0 \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$.
  \end{enumerate}
  So all incoming edges onto nodes in $W$ become incoming edges into the corresponding nodes in $W^0$,
  all outgoing edges out of nodes in $W$ become outgoing edges out of the corresponding nodes in $W^1$,
  and edges $w^0 \tuh w^1$ are added for all nodes in $W$.
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [disjointness_of_new_copies_only_partially_stipulated] The disjointness assertions $W^0 \cap V = W^1 \cap V = W^0 \cap J = W^1 \cap J = W^0 \cap W^1 = \emptyset$ are to be realized at the type level: in a formal encoding, $W^0$ and $W^1$ are constructed as tagged copies (e.g. via a $\Sigma$-type or tagged sum) so that their elements are of a distinct type from elements of $V$ and $J$, making disjointness hold automatically rather than as a side condition. The convention $v^0 := v^1 := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ is purely notational shorthand used inside the definitions of $E_{\spl(W)}$ and $L_{\spl(W)}$, and does not reassign these elements to the tagged copy types.

A reader might reasonably expect bidirected edges to follow an "incoming/outgoing" pattern analogous to directed edges, or to be duplicated symmetrically across both copies. The text never states *why* $W^0$ is chosen over $W^1$ (the SWIG-style semantic interpretation — confounding belongs on the "natural"/observational side, while the "intervened" side $W^1$ is causally isolated — is not made explicit). rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Node-splitting hard intervention on CADMGs]
  \label{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CADMG and $W \ins V$ a subset of the output nodes.
    The \emph{single-world intervention graph (SWIG)} w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CADMG: 
    \[ G_{\swig(W)} :=\lp J_{\swig(W)}, V_{\swig(W)}, E_{\swig(W)},L_{\swig(W)} \rp,\]
    %\[ G_{\swig(W)} := G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    %\[G((V\sm W) \dcup W^o|\doit(W^i \dcup J)):=G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    constructed as follows.
    We first make two disjont copies of the nodes in $W$:
    \[ W^o:=\lC w^o\st w \in W \rC, \qquad W^i:=\lC w^i \st w \in W \rC.  \]
    Note that we consider $w^o \neq w^i$ for $w \in W$.
  However, for brevity, for $v \in J \cup V \sm W$ we put:
  \[ v^o:=v^i:=v.  \]
  We then define:
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
      \item $J_{\swig(W)} := J \dcup W^i$,
      \item $V_{\swig(W)} := (V \sm W) \dcup W^o$, %which could be identified with $V$ again if we want to make the identification $W=W^o$.
      \item $E_{\swig(W)} := \lC v^i_1 \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E  \rC$,
      \item $L_{\swig(W)} :=\lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$.
  \end{enumerate}
  where we turn all nodes of $W^i$ into input nodes, removing all edges into $W^i$,
  and we turn all nodes of $W^o$ into output nodes, removing all edges out of $W^o$.
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}

wording_check.output:
Looking carefully at the tex block for def_3_12 (NodeSplittingHard / SWIG).

SUBTLETY:
id: implicit_disjointness_of_copies_from_original_nodes
explanation: The LN introduces $W^o$ and $W^i$ as "two disjont copies of the nodes in $W$" and explicitly states "$w^o \neq w^i$ for $w \in W$", but never asserts that these new symbols are disjoint from the original node set $J \cup V$. Specifically, the definition does not state $W^o \cap (V \setminus W) = \emptyset$, $W^o \cap J = \emptyset$, $W^i \cap V = \emptyset$, or $W^i \cap J = \emptyset$.

This matters because the constructions
\[ J_{\swig(W)} := J \dcup W^i \qquad V_{\swig(W)} := (V \sm W) \dcup W^o \]
use disjoint-union notation $\dcup$, which presumes the operands are already disjoint. A literal reading admits the pathological case where, say, some symbol $w^o$ happens to coincide with an existing $v \in V \setminus W$ — in which case the convention "$v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J \cup V \sm W$" collides with the fresh-copy interpretation (the same symbol $v$ would simultaneously be $v^o$ for "itself" AND $w^o$ for some $w \in W$), making the entire indexing scheme inconsistent.

Similarly the LN's $L_{\swig(W)} := \{v_1^o \huh v_2^o : \dots\}$ implicitly relies on $v \mapsto v^o$ being a well-defined injection on $V$ — true under the natural intent but not derivable from the literal text.

Standard treatments fix this by saying "fresh copies disjoint from the underlying universe"; the LN omits that qualifier.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: closing_remark_uses_removal_language_for_a_constructive_definition
explanation: The closing sentence — "where we turn all nodes of $W^i$ into input nodes, removing all edges into $W^i$, and we turn all nodes of $W^o$ into output nodes, removing all edges out of $W^o$" — describes the SWIG as if it were produced by *removing* edges from some intermediate graph. But the actual construction in clauses (iii)–(iv) is a direct mapping:
\[ E_{\swig(W)} := \{v_1^i \tuh v_2^o : v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E\}, \qquad L_{\swig(W)} := \{v_1^o \huh v_2^o : v_1 \huh v_2 \in L\}. \]
No edges are ever included and then removed; the mapping itself ensures no edge ends at $W^i$ (since targets are always tagged $\cdot^o$) and no directed edge leaves $W^o$ (since sources are always tagged $\cdot^i$). A reader taking the remark literally might suspect there is a separate edge-deletion step they should perform, or that some original edges are silently discarded — neither of which is true. This is descriptive imprecision rather than mathematical error, but is the kind of phrasing that produces "what are we removing exactly?" confusion downstream.

Additionally, the word "edges" in "removing all edges out of $W^o$" is ambiguous between directed and bidirected: clause (iv) does place bidirected edges incident to $W^o$, so the remark must implicitly mean "directed edges out of $W^o$" — but the LN never disambiguates. If a downstream argument cites this clause to claim "$W^o$ has no edges except parents", that would be wrong with respect to $L_{\swig(W)}$.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: ExtendingCDMGsWith
rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Extending CDMGs with intervention nodes]\label{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}
  Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes.
  The \emph{extended CDMG} of $G$ w.r.t.\ nodes $W \ins J \cup V$  and corresponding \emph{intervention nodes} 
%  $I_W = \{I_w \,|\, w \in W\}$ \Joris{with $I_j:=j$ for $j \in J$},
  $I_W = \{I_w \,|\, w \in W\}$ with $I_j:=j$ for $j \in J \cap W$,
  is the CDMG: 
  \[ G_{\doit(I_W)}:=(J_{\doit(I_W)},V_{\doit(I_W)},E_{\doit(I_W)},L_{\doit(I_W)}),\]
  %\[G(V|\doit(J \dcup I_W)):=G_{\doit(I_W)}=(J_{\doit(I_W)},V_{\doit(I_W)},E_{\doit(I_W)},L_{\doit(I_W)}),\]
  where:
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
%      \item $J_{\doit(I_W)}:= J\, \dot{\cup} \, \lC I_w\,|\, w \in W \Joris{\sm J} \rC$, %not W \sm J, makes weak and hard interventions commute
      \item $J_{\doit(I_W)}:= J\, \dot{\cup} \, \lC I_w\,|\, w \in W \sm J \rC$, %not W \sm J, makes weak and hard interventions commute
      \item $V_{\doit(I_W)}:= V$,
      \item $E_{\doit(I_W)}:= E\, \dot{\cup} \, \lC I_w \tuh w \,|\, w \in W\sm J \rC$,
      \item $L_{\doit(I_W)}:= L$,
  \end{enumerate}
%  where we just add \Joris{nodes $I_w$ for $w \in W \sm J$ and} edges $I_w \tuh w$ for $ w \in W \sm J$. 
  where we just add nodes $I_w$ for $w \in W \sm J$ and edges $I_w \tuh w$ for $ w \in W \sm J$.
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [I_W_mixes_fresh_nodes_and_existing_context_nodes] Intervention nodes are by default fresh nodes disjoint from $J \cup V$, not present in $G$ prior to the intervention. For $w \in W \sm J$, $I_w$ is such a new symbol, added to the junction set with a single outgoing edge $I_w \tuh w$. For $w \in J \cap W$, the identification $I_w := w$ introduces no new node and serves only as a notational convenience so that $I_W$ is well-defined as a set; here the pre-existing context node $w \in J$ retains whatever edges $G$ already assigned to it.

wording_check.output:
Now I have a clear picture. Let me analyze the active definition (starting line 799) for subtleties.

The L issue I initially considered is NOT a real problem — L is bidirected edges ⊆ V×V, and V is unchanged. But I do see a concrete subtlety in how the new intervention nodes are introduced.

SUBTLETY:
id: fresh_intervention_nodes_not_required_disjoint_from_V
explanation: The construction introduces new intervention nodes via
"$J_{\doit(I_W)}:= J\, \dot{\cup} \, \lC I_w\,|\, w \in W \sm J \rC$"
and the only explicit constraint on the $I_w$ (for $w \in W \sm J$) is what
the disjoint-union symbol $\dot\cup$ enforces: disjointness from $J$. The
text never explicitly requires $I_w \notin V$, nor that distinct $w \ne w'$
yield distinct $I_w \ne I_{w'}$. The clause "$I_j := j$ for $j \in J \cap W$"
pins down $I_w$ only on $J \cap W$; for $w \in W \sm J \subseteq V$, the LN
simply uses the symbol $I_w$ without ever saying it is fresh.

Why this matters: a CDMG requires $J' \cap V' = \emptyset$ (see def-cdmg
line 35-40, where $J$ and $V$ are required to be disjoint). The literal
reading admits a pathological assignment such as "$I_w := w$ for $w \in
W \sm J \subseteq V$": that satisfies $\{I_w\}\cap J = \emptyset$ (so the
$\dot\cup$ in i.) is fine) but it makes $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cap V_{\doit(I_W)}
= W \cap V \ne \emptyset$ (whenever $W$ hits $V$), so the result is not a
valid CDMG. Similarly, "$I_{w_1}=I_{w_2}$" for distinct $w_1, w_2 \in W
\sm J$ is not ruled out, and would make the new edges $I_{w_1}\tuh w_1$
and $I_{w_2}\tuh w_2$ share a source that the LN clearly intends to keep
separate.

Concrete corner case: take $W = V$ with $V = \{v_1, v_2\}$, and let the
reader set $I_{v_1} := v_2$, $I_{v_2} := v_1$. Both are outside $J$, so
the disjoint union in i.) is well-defined. But then $J_{\doit(I_W)} = J
\cup \{v_1, v_2\} = J \cup V$, $V_{\doit(I_W)} = V$, and $J' \cap V' = V
\ne \emptyset$. Equally the new "edges" $v_2 \tuh v_1$ and $v_1 \tuh v_2$
may already be in $E$, making $E \dot\cup \{\ldots\}$ also ill-defined.

What the author plainly intends — judging from the surrounding prose "we
just add nodes $I_w$ for $w \in W \sm J$" — is that each $I_w$ for $w \in
W \sm J$ is a brand-new symbol distinct from anything in $J \cup V$ and
from any other $I_{w'}$. But the formal text only states disjointness
from $J$.

Downstream impact: any claim about the extended CDMG (e.g. about
$|J_{\doit(I_W)}|$, about acyclicity, about $I_w$ being a parentless
"source", or about adding/removing intervention edges commuting with
other operations) silently relies on this freshness. A formalization
will need to commit to a specific freshness mechanism (e.g.\ a sum-type
construction, or an explicit "fresh" hypothesis), since the LN does not
supply one.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: AcyclicHardInterventionTopologicalOrder

wording_check.output:
I've analyzed the tex block. The remark makes three claims: (1) acyclicity is preserved, (2) a topological order on $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is one for $G$, (3) any topological order of $G$ can be extended, with the example "putting all $I_w$ first". Combined with `def_3_13`, which explicitly permits $W \cap J \neq \emptyset$ and sets $I_w := w$ for $w \in J \cap W$, several subtleties arise.

```
SUBTLETY:
id: all_I_w_first_clashes_with_J_cap_W_nonempty
explanation: The example "by putting all the $I_w$ nodes first in the ordering" reads ambiguously
once one inspects the underlying def_3_13. The set $I_W$ is defined there as
$I_W = (J \cap W) \cup \{I_w : w \in W \setminus J\}$, with the explicit convention "for
$j \in J \cap W$ we put $I_j := j$ purely as a notational shorthand: the symbol $I_j$ is a label
for the pre-existing input node $j \in J$ itself, and \emph{no new node is introduced on the
$J \cap W$ branch}". So a literal reading of "all the $I_w$ nodes" includes the pre-existing
$J$-nodes for $w \in J \cap W$.

Corner case where this bites: let $W \cap J \neq \emptyset$, e.g.\ $G$ has $J = \{j_1, j_2\}$,
$V = \{v_1, v_2\}$ with edges $j_1 \to v_1, j_2 \to v_2, v_1 \to v_2$, and take $W = \{j_2, v_1\}$.
Then $I_W = \{j_2, I_{v_1}\}$ (the first is the pre-existing $j_2$; the second is fresh). The
input topological order on $G$ could be $j_1 < v_1 < j_2 < v_2$. "Putting all the $I_w$ nodes
first" would force $j_2$ and $I_{v_1}$ to the very front, producing something like
$I_{v_1} < j_2 < j_1 < v_1 < v_2$. This is a valid topological order on $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, but its
restriction to $J \cup V$ is $j_2 < j_1 < v_1 < v_2$, which is \emph{not} the input order we
started from. So the example does not literally "extend" the given topological order of $G$ — it
reshuffles part of it.

The author almost certainly means "put just the fresh $I_w$ (those for $w \in W \setminus J$)
first", which does extend correctly because the fresh nodes are by construction disjoint from
$J \cup V$. But because def_3_13 went out of its way to say $I_w$ is a notation defined on
\emph{all} of $W$, the literal phrase "all the $I_w$ nodes" is the broader set.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: extend_meaning_unspecified
explanation: The remark uses "extended" without defining what extending a topological order means
in this setting. Standard mathematical usage takes "extension of an order" to require that the
new (strict total) order, when restricted to the original ground set, equals the original order.
Under that reading, "putting all the $I_w$ nodes first" is the problematic example documented in
the subtlety above — when $W \cap J$ is non-empty, the restriction can differ from the original
order. A weaker reading of "extend" (e.g.\ "produce some topological order on $G_{\doit(I_W)}$
whose underlying ground set includes the nodes of $G$") makes the example trivially correct but
makes the word "extend" essentially vacuous: any topological order on $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is then an
"extension" of any topological order on $G$. The text gives no hint which reading is intended,
and only the strict reading would forbid the corner case above.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: top_order_node_set_mismatch
explanation: The phrase "a topological order for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is also one for $G$" reads
loosely. By def_3_8 a topological order of $G$ is a strict total order on $J \cup V$, and one for
$G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is a strict total order on $J \cup V \cup \{I_w : w \in W \setminus J\}$. When
$W \setminus J \neq \emptyset$ those node sets are not equal, so the relation literally given as a
topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is \emph{not} a relation on $J \cup V$ — its restriction
is. This is the kind of standard informal abuse a domain expert glosses over, but a strict
formalization needs to insert a restriction step that the LN does not name. The same
informality reappears in reverse in "extended", as flagged separately.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: order_among_I_w_unspecified
explanation: "putting all the $I_w$ nodes first in the ordering" leaves the relative order
\emph{among} the $I_w$ nodes themselves unspecified. This is harmless on the merits — fresh
$I_w$ nodes are pairwise unrelated in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (each has a single outgoing edge to its
target $w \in V$ and no incoming edges, and there are no edges between distinct $I_w, I_{w'}$),
so any internal ordering is fine — but the text neither states this nor selects a representative.
Worth flagging only because a formalization must pick a concrete construction (e.g.\ an arbitrary
enumeration of $W \setminus J$) where the prose stays silent.
END_SUBTLETY
```

rows.title: AddingInterventionNodes
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [doit_overloaded_for_node_addition_vs_hard_intervention] The notation $G_{\doit(\cdot)}$ is overloaded in this lemma and is disambiguated by the type of its argument. For $W \subseteq J \cup V$, the expression $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ denotes the operation of adding the intervention nodes $I_W$ to $G$, whereas $G_{\doit(W)}$ denotes the standard hard intervention on $W$ (severing incoming edges into $W$). When both an intervention-node set and a pre-existing-node set appear together within a single $\doit(\cdot)$, as in $G_{\doit(I_{W_1}, W_2)}$, the notation denotes the composition of the two operations.

wording_check.output:
Now I have enough context. Let me audit the literal text of claim_3_14.

**Analysis:**

Def 3.13 takes $W \ins J \cup V$ (with $I_j := j$ for $j \in J \cap W$, and new nodes only for $W \sm J$).
Def 3.10 (hard intervention) also takes $W \ins J \cup V$.
So the hypothesis is internally consistent with both upstream definitions.

The first equation is unambiguous — both sides use only $\doit(I_\cdot)$.

The second equation introduces a notation that does not appear earlier in the LN.

```
SUBTLETY:
id: ad_hoc_mixed_doit_notation_g_doit_iw1_comma_w2
explanation: The second equation uses the notation $G_{\doit(I_{W_1}, W_2)}$, which is introduced here for the first time and is *not* defined anywhere in the LN — neither by Def~3.10 (hard intervention, which takes a single set $W$) nor by Def~3.13 (intervention-node extension, which takes a single set $W$). The lemma is the sole specification of this notation, and its meaning is given only implicitly via the equation
\[ \lp G_{\doit(I_{W_1})} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_1})} =  G_{\doit(I_{W_1}, W_2)}. \]
A literal reader has to infer that the comma in "$\doit(I_{W_1}, W_2)$" splits two semantically different operations: $I_{W_1}$ (intervention-node addition for $W_1$, per Def~3.13) and $W_2$ (hard intervention on $W_2$, per Def~3.10). The reader cannot, for example, infer this from the first equation, where "$\doit(I_{W_1 \cup W_2})$" denotes a *single* intervention-node operation on the union and has no comma in its argument. Contrast this with naive parsings the syntax admits:
\begin{itemize}
\item "$\doit$ on the set $I_{W_1} \cup W_2$" (treating the comma as $\cup$),
\item "$\doit(I_{W_1, W_2})$" with an indexed set $I_{W_1, W_2}$,
\item Both $I_{W_1}$ \emph{and} $W_2$ treated as intervention-node operations (since the same $\doit$ symbol covers both kinds elsewhere).
\end{itemize}
None of these are what is meant — the proof and the asymmetric roles of $W_1$ and $W_2$ in the LHS/middle force the intended reading. Downstream uses of "$G_{\doit(I_{W_1}, W_2)}$" (if any) must remember that this is shorthand for "first $\doit(I_{W_1})$, then $\doit(W_2)$" (or vice versa, since the lemma proves they commute). Any formalization will need to either (a) pick one of the two orderings as the definition and prove the other as an equality, or (b) define a primitive "mixed" operation and prove both single-operation orderings equal it.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: w_subseteq_j_makes_both_operations_near_identity
explanation: The hypothesis "$W_1, W_2 \ins J \cup V$" literally allows either set to lie entirely in $J$ (i.e.\ to consist solely of input nodes). The literal definitions then make both interventions essentially trivial in this corner case:
\begin{itemize}
\item By Def~3.13 (line~811), $J_{\doit(I_W)} := J \dcup \{I_w \mid w \in W \sm J\}$ and $E_{\doit(I_W)} := E \dcup \{I_w \tuh w \mid w \in W \sm J\}$. For $W \ins J$, $W \sm J = \emptyset$, so $G_{\doit(I_W)} = G$ exactly.
\item By Def~3.10 (lines~299--302), $J_{\doit(W)} := J \cup W$ and $V_{\doit(W)} := V \sm W$. For $W \ins J$, this gives $J \cup W = J$ and $V \sm W = V$ (since $J \cap V = \emptyset$), and removing "incoming edges into $W$" is also vacuous if the CDMG has no edges into $J$.
\end{itemize}
Consequently, for, e.g., $W_1 \ins J$ with $W_2 \ins V$, the first equation reads $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} = G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} = G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ — trivially true — and similarly for the second equation. A reader expecting the lemma to assert a non-trivial commutativity for *all* $W_1, W_2 \ins J \cup V$ does not get more than the $W \sm J$ content; the $W \cap J$ portion contributes nothing. The literal text does not flag this, and there is no companion hypothesis "$W_1, W_2 \ins V$" (which would be the truly load-bearing case). This is not strictly incorrect, but a formalization that relies on the lemma being "tight" on $J \cup V$ inputs should note that the substantive content reduces to $W_i \sm J$.
END_SUBTLETY
```

[bifurcation_index_boundary_excludes_natural_cases] The "bifurcation" in (iv) is to be interpreted according to the previously formalized definition of bifurcation, not by the literal index range $w_1, \dots, w_{n-1}$ displayed in the walk. id: bifurcation_indexing_unclear_at_k_boundaries

    \[\ul{v} \hut w_1 \hut \cdots \hut w_{k-1} \hus w_k \tuh \cdots \tuh w_{n-1} \tuh \ol{v}, \]

explanation: Item iii ($E^{\sm W}$) explicitly allows self-loops, with the footnote "Note that this may introduce self-cycles." Item iv ($L^{\sm W}$), by contrast, requires "$\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$", silently excluding self-bidirected edges that bifurcations through $W$ would otherwise create.

Concrete corner case: take $v \in V \sm W$ and $w_1, w_2 \in W$ such that $G$ contains the structure
    $v \hut w_1 \hus w_2 \tuh v$ (i.e., $w_1 \tuh v$, $w_1 \hus w_2$, and $w_2 \tuh v$ all in $G$).
This is a bona-fide bifurcation in the sense of the displayed formula, with $\ul{v} = \ol{v} = v$, $k = 2$, $n = 2$, and intermediates $w_1, w_2 \in W$. The natural reading would add a self-bidirected $v \huh v$ to $L^{\sm W}$, capturing "confounding of $v$ with itself through latent path in $W$". The "$\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$" condition discards this case.

The asymmetry with item iii's footnote is jarring: the directed case acknowledges self-cycle creation as a feature, while the bidirected case rules it out without justification in the definition itself. If the surrounding CDMG framework forbids self-bidirected edges by convention, that justifies the asymmetry — but the definition doesn't say so. Downstream proofs about which confounding paths are "preserved" by marginalization should account for the fact that confounding paths returning to their starting node are silently dropped, while directed cycles returning to the starting node are kept.
END_SUBTLETY
```

rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[bifurcation_optional_v3_phrasing_ambiguity] Part 2 of the remark asserts two separate biconditionals, corresponding to reading the statement once with the parenthetical clauses omitted and once with them included. Explicitly:
(a) For $v_1, v_2 \in G \sm W$: there is a bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $G$ if and only if there is a bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $G^{\sm W}$.
(b) For $v_1, v_2, v_3 \in G \sm W$: there is a bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ with source $v_3$ in $G$ if and only if there is a bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ with source $v_3$ in $G^{\sm W}$.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: optionally_v3_phrasing_creates_dual_claim
explanation: Item 2 reads:

> "For $v_1,v_2 \in G \sm W$ (and, optionally, $v_3 \in G\sm W$): there is a bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ (with source $v_3$) in $G$ if and only if there is a bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ (with source $v_3$) in $G^{\sm W}$."

The parenthetical "(and, optionally, $v_3 \in G\sm W$)" combined with the embedded "(with source $v_3$)" makes the statement ambiguous: it bundles two distinct claims into one sentence.

- Reading A — two separate claims: (i) for all $v_1,v_2 \in G \sm W$, an unsourced bifurcation between $v_1,v_2$ exists in $G$ iff in $G^{\sm W}$; (ii) for all $v_1,v_2,v_3 \in G\sm W$, a bifurcation between $v_1,v_2$ with source $v_3$ exists in $G$ iff in $G^{\sm W}$.
- Reading B — a single claim where $v_3$ is universally quantified and the "(with source $v_3$)" is mandatory wherever it appears.

"Optionally" is mathematically unusual; a formalization (or a careful reader) has to commit to one interpretation, and the two readings are not the same claim. In particular, reading A includes the unsourced version with its existential over sources, which is the strictly stronger statement.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: bifurcation_no_source_existential_admits_w_source
explanation: Under reading A of item 2 (the "(with source $v_3$)" optional), the unsourced version reads "bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $G$ iff bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $G^{\sm W}$" with $v_1,v_2 \in G \sm W$. The LHS implicitly quantifies the source existentially over all of $V(G)$, including $W$. The RHS quantifies over $V(G^{\sm W}) = V(G)\sm W$, which by construction contains no $W$ vertices.

Concrete corner case: suppose the only bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $G$ is the fork $v_1 \leftarrow w \rightarrow v_2$ with $w \in W$ (no other common-source vertex in $V(G)$, no bidirected edge in $G$). The LHS is true. For the RHS to also be true, $G^{\sm W}$ must contain something that counts as a "bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$" — typically the bidirected edge $v_1 \leftrightarrow v_2$ that marginalization is meant to introduce.

So the unsourced biconditional silently relies on: (a) "bifurcation" being defined inclusively enough to count bidirected edges as bifurcations (with an implicit unobserved source), and (b) the marginalization $G \mapsto G^{\sm W}$ actually inserting such bidirected edges for marginalized common causes. If the document's local notion of "bifurcation" is the strict three-vertex fork $v_1 \leftarrow v_3 \rightarrow v_2$ with $v_3$ a vertex of the graph, then under that strict reading the unsourced direction fails on this corner case, and a downstream proof that walks through the unsourced biconditional would silently rely on the more permissive definition.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: MarginalizationsCommute

wording_check.output:
Looking at the claim carefully:

The lemma states that for a CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ and two disjoint subsets $W_1, W_2 \subseteq V$, the iterated marginalizations $(G^{\sm W_1})^{\sm W_2}$, $(G^{\sm W_2})^{\sm W_1}$, and the joint marginalization $G^{\sm (W_1 \cup W_2)}$ are all equal.

Let me check potential issues:

1. **Type safety of nested marginalization**: For $(G^{\sm W_1})^{\sm W_2}$ to be well-defined, $W_2$ must be a subset of the output nodes of $G^{\sm W_1}$, namely $V \setminus W_1$. The disjointness assumption $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ combined with $W_2 \subseteq V$ ensures this. ✓

2. **Degenerate cases**:
   - $W_1 = W_2 = \emptyset$: Two empty sets are disjoint; the claim reduces to $G = G = G$. ✓
   - $W_1 = \emptyset$, $W_2 \neq \emptyset$: Reduces to $G^{\sm W_2} = G^{\sm W_2} = G^{\sm W_2}$. ✓
   - $W_1 = V$: After marginalizing, output set is empty; $W_2 \subseteq V$ disjoint from $V$ forces $W_2 = \emptyset$. ✓

3. **Internal consistency**: The notation $G^{\sm (W_1 \cup W_2)}$ is well-defined since $W_1 \cup W_2 \subseteq V$.

4. **"Output nodes"**: The terminology is consistent with the CDMG convention where $V$ are the output (observed) nodes and $J$ the intervention nodes.

5. **Disjointness**: "Two disjoint subsets" unambiguously means $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$.

The wording is clean. Whether the equality actually holds is a proof concern (it depends on the marginalization definition producing a canonical graph regardless of order), but the *statement* itself is unambiguous about what is being asserted.

NO_SUBTLETIES

wording_check.output:
Looking at the tex block for claim_3_19, I'll analyze the literal wording for subtleties.

The lemma states an isomorphism between $G_{\doit(W)}$ and $\lp G_{\swig(W)}\rp^{\sm W^o}$ via the map "$w \mapsto w^i$". Let me check this carefully against possible corner cases and ambiguities.

SUBTLETY:
id: isomorphism_map_unspecified_outside_W
explanation: The lemma writes the isomorphism as

  "$G_{\doit(W)} \cong \lp G_{\swig(W)}\rp^{\sm W^o}, \qquad w \mapsto w^i$"

but the literal text "$w \mapsto w^i$" only specifies the action of the map on $w \in W$. The full isomorphism is between two CDMGs whose node sets do not coincide setwise:

- $G_{\doit(W)}$ has node set (presumably) $J \cup V$ — hard intervention typically preserves nodes.
- $\lp G_{\swig(W)}\rp^{\sm W^o}$ has node set $J \cup (V \setminus W) \cup W^i$ — after splitting $W$ into $W^i, W^o$ and removing $W^o$.

The action of the bijection on $V \setminus W$ (presumably the identity $v \mapsto v$) and on $J$ (presumably identity) is left implicit. A reader has to supply this by convention. This is standard math shorthand, but the literal text is genuinely under-specified, and any downstream formal artifact will need to make the identity-on-the-complement explicit to define a well-formed bijection.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: output_node_precondition_relies_on_external_definition
explanation: The precondition reads

  "Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W \ins V$ be a subset of output nodes from $G$."

The phrase "subset of output nodes from $G$" presupposes a defined input/output distinction within $V$ (since $W$ is restricted to a proper sub-class of $V$, not all of $V$). The literal text of this lemma does not internally specify what counts as an "output node" — it relies on the CDMG definition elsewhere. Two readings are possible:

(a) $V$ already partitions into input nodes and output nodes; $W$ must be drawn from the output side only.
(b) "Output node" is a property assigned via the labels $L$ and the restriction is about that labeling.

If the SWIG/hard-intervention operations are only well-defined for "output" nodes, this restriction is load-bearing; if not, the precondition is a meaningful constraint and silently violating it (passing an input node in $W$) would produce ill-defined right-hand side $W^o$. The lemma does not flag this consequence; a literal reader who has a slightly different notion of "output node" might miss the precondition's force.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: CollidersAndNon

wording_check.output:
I have enough context. Let me also check if `def_3_4` constrains away self-loops in walks.

The walk definition explicitly allows $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$ with the possibility that $v_k = v_{k+1}$ (no distinctness requirement). The CDMG def only forbids self-loops in $L$, not in $E$. The walk also allows "trivial" walks ($n=0$).

Based on this analysis, here is my assessment of the tex block of `def_3_15`:

SUBTLETY:
id: self_loop_makes_tuh_and_hut_simultaneously_true
explanation: A directed self-loop $(v,v) \in E$ is permitted by the CDMG definition (`def-cdmg`: $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ contains no $v \ne v$ exclusion — only $L$ explicitly forbids self-loops). Per the notation `def_3_2`, the macros decode to $v_1 \tuh v_2 \iff (v_1, v_2) \in E$ and $v_1 \hut v_2 \iff (v_2, v_1) \in E$. So for a self-loop, $v \tuh v$ and $v \hut v$ are **both** true simultaneously, since $(v,v) = (v,v)$.

Now consider a walk position $k$ where $a_{k-1}$ is a directed self-loop at $v$ (so $v_{k-1} = v_k = v$) and $a_k$ is a directed edge into $v_k$ from $v_{k+1}$ (so $v_k \hut v_{k+1}$ and thus $v_k \hus v_{k+1}$). The literal tex says position $k$ is a non-collider iff it falls into one of the listed cases, and a collider iff it has the collider form. We compute:

- "left chain" $v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}$: the self-loop satisfies $v_{k-1} \hut v_k$ (as shown above), and $v_k \hus v_{k+1}$ holds. **Matches → non-collider.**
- "collider" $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1}$: the self-loop also satisfies $v_{k-1} \tuh v_k$, which implies $v_{k-1} \suh v_k$ (since $\suh \iff \tuh \lor \huh$). And $v_k \hus v_{k+1}$ holds. **Matches → collider.**

So a single position is *literally classified as both a non-collider and a collider* by the block, breaking the implicit mutual exclusivity that the wording ("if it falls into **one** of the following cases" vs. "is **of the form**") relies on. The mark-count gloss "i.e.\ if there is at most one arrowhead pointing towards $v_k$" / "i.e.\ if there are two arrowheads pointing towards $v_k$" is also ambiguous for self-loops: the loop has its arrowhead and its tail both anchored at $v_k$, and the tex never specifies whether that contributes 0, 1, or 2 to the count.

This is not a vacuous corner: the recent "Refactor_marginalize_loose_self_cycle" work in this codebase confirms self-cycles are actively handled, and downstream notions (`def_3_16` blockable/unblockable, `def_3_17` $\sigma$-blocked walks, claim_3_25 / claim_3_27, etc.) inherit this ambiguity for any walk that traverses a self-loop. A faithful encoding will have to pick a tie-breaking convention (e.g. read the walk's $a_{k-1}$ as carrying a *directed* orientation $(v_{k-1}, v_k)$ vs $(v_k, v_{k-1})$ and use only the $v_k$-side mark of that ordered pair), but the literal tex does not commit to one.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: arrowhead_count_scope_implicit_not_stated
explanation: The non-collider definition says "if there is at most one arrowhead pointing towards $v_k$" with no explicit restriction to the walk's two incident edges $a_{k-1}, a_k$. By contrast, the collider half says "if there are two arrowheads pointing towards $v_k$ **on the walk $\pi$**" — the qualifier "on the walk $\pi$" appears in the collider gloss but is absent in the non-collider gloss. A strictly literal reading of the non-collider clause could count arrowheads from *every* edge of $G$ incident to $v_k$ (including non-walk edges); under that reading an end-node $v_0$ that has, say, three other incoming edges in $E$ outside the walk would fail "at most one arrowhead" yet still be classified as a non-collider by the end-node bullet — contradicting the "i.e." equivalence. The intended scope is clearly walk-adjacent, but the tex relies on the reader inferring this from the leading "on $\pi$" qualifier rather than stating it consistently with the collider half.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.tips:

Refactored on 2026-06-19 via `cdmg_typed_edges` (see leanification/Chapter*/Refactor_cdmg_typed_edges_DONE_*/refactor_data.json).

wording_check.output:
Now I have full context. The CDMG has edges $E$ (directed) and $L$ (bidirected — symmetric, two heads). The shorthand symbols (`\hut`, `\hus`, etc.) per def_3_2 are EXISTENCE claims about edges in $G$, not statements about the walk's specific edges. And def_3_15 explicitly reconciled this ambiguity for the collider/non-collider classification, but def_3_16 does not.

Let me write my audit.

SUBTLETY:
id: pattern_shorthands_existential_in_g_not_walk_specific
explanation: The unblockable patterns "$v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}$", "$v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1}$", and "$v_{k-1} \hut v_k \tuh v_{k+1}$" use shorthand defined in def_3_2 as *existence* claims about edges in $G$ (e.g. $v_{k-1} \hut v_k :\iff (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$). The verbal characterization that precedes the patterns — "it only has outgoing edges **on $\pi$** to nodes in the same strongly connected component of $G$" — is about the walk's *specific* incident edges $a_{k-1}, a_k$.

Def_3_15 had to explicitly reconcile this exact mismatch ("the LN writing $v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}$ ... spelled out via the walk constraint, $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$..."), declaring the count-based, walk-specific reading "canonical". Def_3_16 inherits the shorthand without restating this reconciliation.

Concrete corner case: suppose between adjacent walk-positions $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ the graph contains *both* $(v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ and $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$, and the walk uses $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1})$. Suppose further $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$, so on the walk $v_k$ is a fork (count $= 0$, both walk-incident edges out of $v_k$). Now suppose only $v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ while $v_{k+1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$. Walk-based reading: $v_k$ has an outgoing walk-edge ($a_k$) to a different SC component, so it should be blockable. But existential pattern reading: $v_{k-1} \hut v_k$ holds (one of the $E$-edges supplies it) and $v_k \hus v_{k+1}$ requires $(v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$ or $(v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$. If either of those exists (e.g. add a bidirected edge $(v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$ to the example), the "left chain" pattern matches existentially and the condition $v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ is met — so the literal pattern classification calls $v_k$ unblockable, contradicting the walk-based intent.

Downstream this matters for any proof about $\sigma$-blocking (def_3_17 cites this definition): a formalization that follows the patterns literally will disagree with one that follows the verbal "outgoing edges on $\pi$" characterization, exactly on positions whose walk-incident edges differ from other edges that the shorthand existence-quantifies over.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: self_loop_pattern_overlap_inherited
explanation: Def_3_15 explicitly warns that at a directed self-loop traversed by a walk — i.e. at a position $k$ with $v_{k-1} = v_k$ via $(v,v) \in E$ — "a single interior position $k$ traversing a directed self-loop can simultaneously satisfy the 'left chain', 'right chain', 'fork', and 'collider' pattern conjunctions" under the literal def_3_2 reading of $\hut, \tuh, \hus, \suh$. Def_3_16 reuses these same patterns ("left chain", "right chain", "fork") without restating this caveat, so the same overlap occurs here. Concretely: if $v_{k-1} = v_k = v$ with $(v,v) \in E$, then $v_{k-1} \hut v_k$ and $v_k \tuh v_{k-1}$ collapse to the same condition $(v,v) \in E$, and $v \in \Sc^G(v)$ trivially, so the "fork" unblockable pattern matches automatically — even though the walk's actual classification (per def_3_15's canonical count-based reading) might be collider. Whether $v_k$ ends up "unblockable non-collider" thus depends on which reading governs def_3_16; the text doesn't pick one explicitly.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: blockable_clause_says_arrow_not_outgoing_edge
explanation: The "blockable" elaboration says "it has at least one outgoing arrow $v_k \tuh v_{k\pm 1}$ pointing to a node $v_{k\pm 1}$ that lies in a different strongly connected component". Per def_3_2 item 2, $v_k \tuh v_{k\pm 1}$ is the *existence* of $(v_k, v_{k\pm 1}) \in E$ in $G$, regardless of whether that directed edge is the walk's specific incident edge at position $k$.

So under the literal reading: $v_k$ can have an outgoing arrow $v_k \tuh v_{k-1}$ in $G$ to a node $v_{k-1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$ even if the walk's $a_{k-1}$ is actually $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$ (into $v_k$). The blockable condition would then literally be satisfied via an edge that is not the walk's edge. Combined with the symmetric ambiguity in the unblockable patterns, $v_k$ could literally satisfy both "unblockable left chain" (via some auxiliary edge configuration meeting same-SC) and "blockable via outgoing arrow to non-SC" (via the auxiliary $v_k \tuh v_{k-1}$ to non-SC $v_{k-1}$). The intent is clearly the walk-edge reading, but the text says "outgoing arrow $v_k \tuh v_{k\pm 1}$", not "the walk's $a_{k\pm 1}$ is an outgoing edge from $v_k$ to a node not in $\Sc^G(v_k)$".
END_SUBTLETY

rows.addition_to_the_LN: [acyclic_does_not_imply_directed_in_text] In this remark $G$ is a CDMG, as fixed by the surrounding context; the hypothesis "$G$ is acyclic" is to be read as acyclicity in the CDMG sense, so the edge orientations needed to define (non-)colliders are already part of the ambient structure.

wording_check.output:
Based on my analysis, let me audit this claim carefully.

The claim states: "If $G$ is acyclic then all non-colliders are blockable."

Let me verify by checking the relevant definitions:
- **def_3_15** (CollidersAndNon): non-collider is per-walk (end-node, left chain, right chain, or fork pattern)
- **def_3_16** (BlockableAndUnblockable): unblockable requires non-end-node AND all directed outgoing edges go to $\Sc^G(v_k)$; otherwise blockable
- **def_3_6** (Acyclicity): no non-trivial directed walk from $v$ to itself
- **$\Sc^G(v)$**: $\Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v)$, always contains $v$

In an acyclic CDMG, $\Sc^G(v_k) = \{v_k\}$ (otherwise we'd have a non-trivial directed cycle). For an unblockable non-collider, the outgoing edge $v_k \tuh v_{k\pm1}$ would require $v_{k\pm1} \in \{v_k\}$, i.e., a self-loop — but self-loops are excluded by acyclicity (length-1 directed walk from $v_k$ to itself). End-nodes are trivially blockable. So the claim holds straightforwardly.

The wording uses standard mathematical shorthand: "all non-colliders are blockable" implicitly quantifies over walks and positions (since both notions are per-walk). This is unambiguous given the surrounding definitions.

The edge cases all behave reasonably:
- Walks of length 0/1: end-nodes only, trivially blockable
- Empty graphs / no walks: vacuously true
- Self-loops: excluded by acyclicity

rows.addition_to_the_LN: [claim_type_mismatch_vertex_vs_walk] The trailing claim asserting that unblockable non-colliders are always $C$-$\sigma$-open is excluded from the formalization. It is informal commentary, not part of Definition~\ref{def:sigma_blocking}, and no Lean obligation should be derived from it.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: trailing_claim_applies_sigma_open_to_vertices_not_walks
explanation: The formal definition introduces "C-σ-open" exclusively as a property of WALKS: "We say that the walk π is: ... C-σ-open (or σ-open given C) if and only if [conditions on colliders and blockable non-colliders along π]". However, the embedded claim at the end states:

"Note that unblockable non-colliders are always $C$-$\sigma$-open, regardless of the subset $C \ins V \cup J$."

This applies the predicate "C-σ-open" to *vertices* (specifically, unblockable non-collider occurrences on a walk), not to walks. The formal definition gives no meaning to "a non-collider is C-σ-open" — only walks have been declared open or blocked. So the literal reading is a category/type mismatch with the just-stated definition.

Two plausible intended readings:
(a) "An unblockable non-collider on a walk does not contribute to blocking" — i.e., condition (ii) of σ-blocked only quantifies over BLOCKABLE non-colliders, so unblockable ones are silently ignored. This is the safest reading and is essentially a remark about which vertices appear in condition (ii).
(b) An implicit per-vertex extension of σ-open: a non-collider v_k is σ-open given C iff v_k is unblockable, or (v_k is blockable and v_k ∉ C). Under this reading the walk-level σ-open would decompose as "every collider is in Anc(C) and every non-collider is vertex-level σ-open."

Neither reading is made explicit. A downstream proof or formalization that wants to invoke this remark (e.g., to argue case-by-case along the walk that no individual non-collider blocks, when proving σ-openness of a constructed walk) will have to commit to interpretation (a) or (b). If a future definition or claim writes phrases like "v_k is σ-open" expecting the vertex-level extension (b), this remark is the only textual hook for that reading — and it is not actually a definition.

A secondary, smaller note: the opening line writes "$C \ins J \cup V$" while the trailing claim writes "$C \ins V \cup J$". Same set as a union, but the swapped order is a minor cosmetic inconsistency.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.addition_to_the_LN: [sigma_symmetry_claim_invokes_unstated_reversal_invariance] In a CDMG, a walk is a sequence of incident edges without regard to edge orientation; consequently, every walk from $A$ to $B$ is, read in reverse, a walk from $B$ to $A$, and this reversal is an involution on the set of walks. The symmetry of $\sigma$-separation (when $J = \emptyset$) is to be understood as resting on this walk-reversal involution together with the fact that the $\sigma$-blocking conditions on internal nodes are stated in a manner invariant under reversal.
